stories.body: U.S. Central Command said it carried out a third wave of attacks this week, striking 140 Iranian missile, drone, communications and coastal surveillance sites after Iran’s Revolutionary Guard hit the Cyprus-flagged MV GFS Galaxy while it transited the Strait of Hormuz, leaving the ship powerless and a crew member missing; Tehran responded by declaring the waterway closed, firing on a vessel it said used an unapproved route, launching missiles at a U.S. base in Jordan, and prompting air-defense alerts in the UAE, Qatar, Kuwait and Bahrain as maritime authorities reported the Galaxy’s crew abandoning ship in lifeboats.
stories.headline: US and Iran trade strikes as Tehran shuts the Strait of Hormuz

stories.body: During ceremonies in Mashhad for slain Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Iran’s armed forces said they fired on U.S. Patriot batteries in Kuwait, an early-warning site in Qatar, a fuel depot in Bahrain and Jordan’s Azraq base, part of a broader retaliation to American strikes that Iranian officials said killed 14 people and wounded 78; Tehran also alleged a U.S. projectile hit the perimeter of the Bushehr nuclear plant, while Gulf states reported intercepting missiles and drones as Iran warned that any further U.S. intervention over Hormuz shipping routes would draw a “crushing response.”
stories.headline: Iran claims salvos on US bases across the Gulf as Khamenei is buried

stories.body: European officials are evaluating an Omani proposal that would allow optional navigation fees in the Strait of Hormuz if endorsed by the U.N.’s maritime agency, hoping to fund safe-passage services without imposing compulsory tolls after Iran again closed the corridor; UK ministers warned mandatory charges would be disastrous, U.S. officials pressed Tehran to guarantee civilian shipping, and President Donald Trump alternated between declaring the cease-fire dead and threatening overwhelming retaliation while Iran signaled talks with Oman over adapting Malacca-style rules.
stories.headline: Europe studies non-mandatory navigation fees to keep Hormuz traffic moving

stories.body: Three people familiar with Kremlin deliberations told Reuters that Vladimir Putin has rebuffed advisers urging a cease-fire, believes Russia can soon seize the remaining part of Ukraine’s Donbas region, and is considering broader operations, potentially including strikes on European NATO infrastructure, as Ukraine’s drone attacks on Russian refineries and ports deepen fuel shortages and drive Putin to demand a “security zone” inside Ukraine while public military commentators openly discuss drafting more troops and threatening EU targets.
stories.headline: Sources say Putin preparing new escalation in Ukraine despite peace talk

stories.body: Five astronauts aboard the International Space Station were told to take refuge in their SpaceX Crew Dragon for about two hours after the air leak rate in Russia’s Zvezda module doubled to roughly two pounds per day, prompting a dispute between NASA and Roscosmos over repair methods that involved cutting into hard-to-reach sections; Russia said one of two leaks was sealed and the other posed no immediate threat, and NASA rescinded the evacuation once the repair work paused, underscoring the station’s aging infrastructure as lawmakers debate extending its service life to 2032.
stories.headline: NASA briefly orders ISS shelter-in-place after leak rate doubles

stories.body: Senator Lindsey Graham of South Carolina, a dominant Republican voice on defense policy and one of President Donald Trump’s closest allies despite their bitter 2016 primary, died Saturday evening after what his office called a brief and sudden illness, just days after returning from Kyiv where he met President Volodymyr Zelensky; the four-term lawmaker and current Senate Budget Committee chair had recently negotiated new Russia sanctions with the administration and remained a leading advocate for aggressive U.S. stances toward Iran and Russia.
stories.headline: Senator Lindsey Graham dies suddenly at 71

stories.body: In a 52-47 party-line vote, Senate Republicans advanced a bill sending an extra $70 billion to Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol to finance President Trump’s nationwide deportation crackdown through 2029, defeating Democratic attempts—and some GOP defections—to permanently bar a $1.8 billion “anti-weaponization” fund that critics say could reimburse Trump allies who sue the government; amendments to repurpose or outlaw the fund failed despite pledges from Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche not to use it, leaving House consideration for next week amid broader fights over unrelated White House spending requests.
stories.headline: Senate approves $70 billion ICE boost while keeping Trump’s ‘anti-weaponization’ fund intact

stories.body: State legislators, activists and attorneys across the Deep South told Politico that the Supreme Court’s Louisiana v. Callais ruling, which gutted core protections of the Voting Rights Act, has left them fighting alone as Republicans rush to redraw maps before 2028 while national Democrats prioritize swing states, underfund local parties and clash over whether to counter with aggressive gerrymanders or lawsuits; organizers such as Tennessee Rep. Justin Jones and Florida House Minority Leader Fentrice Driskell say Black communities feel isolated even as the DNC touts training programs, warning that a third of Congressional Black Caucus seats could vanish without a coordinated strategy.
stories.headline: Southern Black leaders warn Democrats they are being abandoned after Voting Rights Act rollback

stories.body: Meteorologists warned that a sprawling high-pressure dome will push triple-digit heat across the Southwest, Great Plains and eventually the East for at least a week, keeping overnight lows 15 to 25 degrees Fahrenheit above normal and denying residents’ bodies any chance to recover, with cities from Bismarck to Bloomington already distributing cooling supplies and the National Weather Service urging people to seek hydration and shade as humidity and Saharan dust worsen heat indices.
stories.headline: Heat dome to engulf two-thirds of the US with dangerous nighttime temperatures

stories.body: Rep. Sylvia Garcia said acting ICE Director David Venturella confirmed that Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, a 35-year Houston resident with no criminal record who was finalizing legal status, was not the intended subject of a Tuesday enforcement operation when a federal agent fatally shot him, intensifying protests and demands for transparency as relatives described him as a homebuilder and community member and mourners held vigils calling for accountability.
stories.headline: Family says man killed by ICE agent in Houston wasn’t a target of the raid

stories.body: A fast-moving blaze that began Thursday near Los Gallardos, Almería has killed at least 12 people—many of them foreign retirees—and scorched between 4,000 and 6,600 hectares as steep terrain, 50 km/h winds and drought-dried scrub trapped residents in dead-end ravines; authorities say eight formal missing-person reports remain active, 1,400 people were evacuated (600 now cleared to return), UME troops are using drones to hunt hotspots, and DNA work is underway to identify victims while most inspected homes remain intact.
stories.headline: Los Gallardos wildfire becomes Andalusia’s deadliest with 12 confirmed dead and 23 missing

stories.body: The European Court of Justice will issue its first judgment Thursday on Spain’s 2024 amnesty law, which has already benefited more than 300 people linked to the Catalan independence process but has stalled for figures like Carles Puigdemont after Spain’s Supreme Court refused to drop embezzlement charges; Madrid, dependent on ERC and Junts support, hopes the Luxembourg ruling will validate the law’s aim of “institutional normalization,” while observers note European tribunals have historically been more sympathetic to the independence movement than Spanish courts.
stories.headline: EU court ruling on July 16 will determine scope of Catalan amnesty law

stories.body: Eight years after Spain welcomed the Aquarius migrants, mainstream conservative leaders are now adopting far-right themes such as “national priority,” with El País chronicling how the assault on Torre Pacheco resident Domingo Tomás by Maghrebi attackers in 2025 became a rallying cry for anti-immigrant mobilization, fueling social media hate campaigns, PP-Vox pacts, and growing fear among migrant workers even as locals recount how their town was built with North African labor and how last year’s retaliatory violence left psychological scars.
stories.headline: Rising xenophobic rhetoric reverberates from Torre Pacheco to national politics

stories.body: A sinkhole eight meters wide and four meters deep opened Tuesday above the L9 metro tunnel works in Barcelona’s Sant Gervasi neighborhood, forcing the evacuation of 93 flats in eight buildings on Teodora Lamadrid, Rubinstein and Sant Gervasi de Cassoles streets; transport officials say residents and shopkeepers must remain out at least until Saturday while three days of ground monitoring, structural inspections and crack comparisons with prior building surveys determine whether a staggered return is safe, with firefighters escorting brief access for belongings.
stories.headline: Sant Gervasi sinkhole tied to L9 tunneling keeps 93 homes evacuated

stories.body: An indefinite strike launched May 26 by CGT and Intersindical workers at the Consorci de Biblioteques de Barcelona has kept roughly 27% of branches closed on average—and as many as 78% during the first week—just as residents seek free climate refuges from extreme heat, with staff maintaining mandatory minimum crews to keep some sites open while the consortium skipped a July 9 mediation session, saying ongoing negotiations were sufficient, and workers warning they may intensify actions in autumn if pay and staffing demands remain unmet.
stories.headline: One in four Barcelona libraries closed amid indefinite strike during heat wave

stories.body: After Barcelona’s Fabra Observatory shattered its all-time record with 40.9°C on July 8, meteorologists expect only a brief weekend respite before a fresh pulse of African air arrives Sunday, driving daytime highs back toward 35°C late next week, pushing inland temperatures beyond 40°C, layering Saharan dust over the city and keeping humidity near 70%, with Meteocat warning that tropical nights above 24–25°C will persist and make sleep difficult despite slightly cooler daytime readings on Saturday.
stories.headline: Third heat wave in a week set to bring Saharan dust and stifling nights to Barcelona

stories.body: The Assemblea Educativa de Catalunya, backed by unions USTEC, CGT, Intersindical, CNT and COS, voted in Sant Celoni to call a public-school strike on September 8—the opening day of the 2026-27 term—arguing that Education Department offers have not addressed chronic understaffing, classroom conditions or demands spanning nursery to vocational tracks, and urging officials to convene urgent bargaining with the strike committee to avoid a disrupted “normal” start.
stories.headline: Education unions plan strike on September 8, first day of Catalan school year
